title: Online Omniprediction
speaker: Sumegha Garg
abstract: A recent line of work has shown a surprising connection between multicalibration, a multi-group fairness notion, and omniprediction, a learning paradigm that provides simultaneous loss minimization guarantees for a large family of loss functions [GKR+22, GHK+23 , GKR23 , GHHK+23]. Prior work studies omniprediction in the batch setting. Our work initiates the study of omniprediction in the online adversarial setting. In the talk, we will briefly see the definitions and motivations for these theoretic notions, and then survey the new results for online omniprediction.

proof_stages.label: Batch prediction and loss-minimization benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up binary-outcome supervised prediction, the Bayes-optimal conditional-probability predictor, and standard loss minimization over a restricted hypothesis class F.

proof_stages.label: Omniprediction with loss-dependent postprocessing
proof_stages.summary: Omniprediction is formulated as learning one probabilistic predictor whose loss-specific Bayes-action postprocessing competes with the best hypothesis in F for every loss in a class, with p* serving as the ideal omnipredictor and agnostic learning as the efficiency benchmark.

proof_stages.label: Multicalibration as a sufficient batch condition
proof_stages.summary: Calibration is strengthened to multicalibration over computationally identifiable groups or functions, and the known theorem is stated that multicalibration with respect to F implies omniprediction for bounded Lipschitz convex losses.

proof_stages.label: Proof sketch of multicalibration implying omniprediction
proof_stages.summary: Under simplifying assumptions of perfect multicalibration and Boolean p*, the proof works level-set by level-set, applies convexity to average each competitor f, uses multicalibration to equate label-conditional averages, and then invokes the optimal postprocessed action.

proof_stages.label: Online adversarial omniprediction and regret
proof_stages.summary: The paper’s online setting replaces iid samples by an adversarial sequence, defines the forecaster–adversary–learner protocol, and asks for one forecast sequence whose loss-specific postprocessings have sublinear regret against F for every loss.

proof_stages.label: Online multicalibration norms and the limits of the l1 route
proof_stages.summary: Online multicalibration errors are measured in l-infinity, l1, and l2 forms; prior l-infinity algorithms can be adapted to l1 rates sufficient for omniprediction, but lower bounds for l1 calibration rule out obtaining square-root regret through this route.

proof_stages.label: Real-valued classes via l2 multicalibration and swap regret
proof_stages.summary: For real-valued or infinite hypothesis classes, the paper obtains sublinear online omniprediction by reducing l2 multicalibration to squared-loss minimization on each forecast level set and implementing this through a swap-regret-style algorithm.

proof_stages.label: Square-root Boolean-class rates and separation from multicalibration
proof_stages.summary: Using a finite characterization of bounded losses by d-losses, Boolean hypothesis classes admit square-root-regret online omniprediction, yielding a separation from l1 multicalibration and leading to open questions about optimal rates for real-valued classes.

examples.name: Squared loss versus absolute loss actions
examples.note: The contrast between squared loss, whose Bayes action is the conditional probability, and absolute loss, whose Bayes action is thresholding at 1/2, shows why omniprediction needs loss-dependent postprocessing rather than using the raw predictor as the action.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the loss-minimizing action for squared loss as p*(x) and contrasts it with the L1/absolute-loss rounding rule at 0.5

passes.reason: ordinary convex losses used to show the naive no-postprocessing omniprediction definition fails because L2 wants the mean while L1 wants rounding
Standard L2/L1 losses are used to refute direct omniprediction without loss-dependent postprocessing.
standard convex losses are contrasted to show the naive omniprediction definition fails without loss-dependent postprocessing
standard squared and absolute losses whose different Bernoulli-optimal actions are used to refute the naive no-postprocessing omniprediction definition
L2 and L1 losses give different optimal postprocessings, refuting the naive single-action omnipredictor definition
The squared- and absolute-loss Bayes actions are respective risk minimizers, and their disagreement refutes the no-postprocessing definition.
The squared-vs-absolute-loss Bayes actions are used to show the naive no-postprocessing omniprediction definition fails.
squared and absolute loss give different Bayes actions, refuting the naive no-postprocessing omniprediction definition
Standard convex-loss actions are contrasted to show the naive no-postprocessing omniprediction definition fails across losses.
Squared and absolute loss are used to show the naive no-postprocessing omniprediction definition fails because their optimal actions differ.
standard squared and absolute losses are used to show the naive no-postprocessing omniprediction definition fails because they require different optimal actions.
standard convex losses whose Bayes actions differ, refuting the naive no-postprocessing omniprediction definition
